common_name: Bachelor's Button Blue Boy (Classic Deep Blue Annual Cornflower)

planting.note: Direct sow in place (bachelor's button does not transplant well); sow in fall for zone 9 winter and spring bloom — seeds germinate readily in cool, moist soil and seedlings tolerate hard frost. In zone 9, sow September–November for bloom January–April. Bachelor's button also responds well to succession sowing every 3 weeks for extended bloom. The brilliant blue color of 'Blue Boy' is among the truest, deepest blues available in garden annuals — a rarer and more saturated blue than most flowers that claim to be blue.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface or barely covered in fall in zone 9; thin to 8–12 inches. Bachelor's Button 'Blue Boy' (also called Cornflower) is a classic annual flower producing deeply pigmented, true-blue, button-shaped double flowers on slender, upright, gray-green stems — one of the few annual flowers that achieves a genuinely vivid blue rather than the bluish-purple that most 'blue' flowers actually are. The deep ultramarine-blue of 'Blue Boy' is the defining color of the species and one of the most sought-after flower colors in gardening. Bachelor's buttons are among the most carefree, self-maintaining annuals in the cool-season garden: they germinate readily in cool, even cold soil; tolerate hard frost; grow without special care in lean soil; and self-seed prolifically to return year after year without replanting. The common name 'Cornflower' reflects the plant's European origin as a weed of grain fields (corn in the British sense, meaning wheat) where the blue flowers appeared among cereal crops — a cultural and agricultural history that makes bachelor's buttons a significant plant in European folk traditions. The flowers make excellent fresh and dried cut flowers, the blue color lasting well when dried.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Bachelor's button Blue Boy variety notes
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Cornflower collection
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Cut-flower annual variety comparison

common_name: Rocket Larkspur (Tall Annual Larkspur, Cool-Season Delphinium Relative)

planting.note: Direct sow in place (larkspur does not transplant well and the taproot is sensitive to disturbance); sow in fall in zone 9 — seed needs cool temperatures (45–55°F) for germination and seeds left on cold soil through winter will germinate when conditions are right. In zone 9, sow October–December for bloom February–April. Larkspur blooms heavily in the cool of late winter and spring and dies when summer heat arrives; it self-seeds reliably and returns each fall. All parts of larkspur are toxic if ingested.

directions: Direct sow seeds in fall on or near the soil surface; do not transplant. Rocket Larkspur (Consolida ajacis, previously Delphinium ajacis) is a tall annual relative of the perennial delphinium — producing the same elegant, towering spikes of densely packed, spurred flowers in shades of blue, purple, lavender, pink, white, and bicolors on upright, 3–5 foot stems, but completing its entire life cycle in one cool season rather than requiring years of perennial establishment. For Gulf South gardeners in zone 8–10, annual larkspur fills the role that perennial delphinium cannot — delphinium is not reliably perennial in the South due to heat and humidity, but annual larkspur thrives in the mild Gulf South winter, blooming prolifically from February through April before summer arrives. The tall, graceful flower spikes are among the most beautiful and romantic elements of the cool-season garden, resembling miniature cottage-garden spires that evoke English country garden aesthetics. Larkspur self-seeds with great reliability — once established in a suitable location, it may naturalize and return each fall from self-sown seed without further planting, creating a self-sustaining annual display over years. All parts are toxic if eaten.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Annual larkspur cut-flower variety comparison
Seed Savers Exchange — Rocket larkspur variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cool-Season Annual Flowers for the Gulf South

common_name: Rocket Snapdragon (Tall Cut-Flower Annual Snapdragon Mix)

planting.note: Start indoors 12–16 weeks before expected transplanting; snapdragon seed is tiny and needs light (surface sow without covering). Chill-treated seeds or germinate at 60–65°F. In zone 9, start seeds in September–October and transplant November–December for bloom January–April — the Gulf South's mild winter is ideal for snapdragons, which bloom in the cool but collapse in summer heat. Rocket is the tall cut-flower type (24–36 inch stems), appropriate for cutting gardens and back of borders.

directions: Sow seeds on the surface of moist, sterile starting mix under bright lights; do not cover — needs light. Transplant after hardening off. Rocket Snapdragon is the tall cut-flower class of Antirrhinum majus — producing long (18–24 inch), sturdy flower stems with densely-packed, spurred flowers in a wide range of colors including red, pink, orange, yellow, white, lavender, and bicolors, ideal for cutting. Snapdragons are quintessential cool-season annuals, performing beautifully in the Gulf South fall through spring garden (September–April in zone 9) and collapsing when summer heat arrives. For zone 9 gardeners, snapdragons are a fall-planted flower that fills the gap between summer annuals and creates a spectacular late-winter and spring cutting garden from November through April — producing abundant long-stemmed flowers during the cool months that rival any season's production. The Rocket series is the premier tall cut-flower snapdragon from PanAmerican Seed, designed specifically for market garden cut-flower production with the longest, straightest stems and best holding quality of any snapdragon class. The individual snap flowers on each spike open from the bottom up, with each flower having the characteristic dragon-snapping mechanism that children (and many adults) find irresistible.
sources: PanAmerican Seed — Rocket snapdragon series introduction
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Cut-flower snapdragon variety comparison
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Annual Flower Production

common_name: Wheat Celosia Flamingo Feather (Slender Spike Cockscomb for Drying)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Flamingo Feather celosia is a spicata type that grows in an elegant, more open, branching habit than cockscomb (cristata) or plume (plumosa) types, producing many narrow, wheat-like spikes per plant. In zone 9, plant April–June. The graceful, narrow spikes are valued for both fresh cutting and dried flower arrangements where the pale pink to rose-pink color and wheat-head form add texture without bulk.

directions: Direct sow 0.125 inch deep after last frost; thin to 8–14 inches. Flamingo Feather is a variety of Celosia argentea var. spicata — the wheat celosia type, distinctly different from the compact cockscomb (var. cristata) and the feathery plume types (var. plumosa). Spicata-type celosias produce many slender, elongated, cylindrical to slightly tapered flower spikes resembling wheat or barley heads, each spike 4–8 inches long, on branching plants that develop an open, airy architecture quite different from the dense, compact forms of other celosia types. Flamingo Feather's color is characteristic: the flower spikes emerge with a rosy-pink to salmon-pink coloration that fades gracefully to pale pink to cream as the spikes age — a color progression that adds visual interest to bouquets and arrangements over time. The wheat-like spikes hold their form beautifully when dried (hang upside down in a dry, dark location) and maintain good color, making Flamingo Feather one of the most valuable annual flowers for dried wreath and arrangement work. Fresh in vases, the elegant, slender spikes provide a soft, feathery texture contrast to the rounder forms of zinnias, sunflowers, and celosias of other types.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Celosia spicata variety notes
Stokes Seeds — Flamingo Feather celosia variety description
Burpee Seeds — Annual flower cut-flower collection

common_name: Stargazer Oriental Lily (Iconic Pink-White Spotted Cut-Flower Bulb Lily)

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall (November–December) in zone 9 for late spring to early summer bloom; orient bulbs pointed end up, 5–6 inches deep. In zone 9, Stargazer performs best when given afternoon shade — the hot summer sun can scorch the blooms and reduce vase quality. In zone 9's warm winters (insufficient chilling), Stargazer may bloom reliably for 1–2 seasons and then decline; for ongoing performance, lift and refrigerate bulbs for 8 weeks at 34–40°F before replanting each fall.

directions: Plant bulbs 5–6 inches deep in fall with pointed end up; mulch over the planting area to moderate soil temperature. Stargazer is the world's most recognized and widely grown Oriental hybrid lily — an F1 hybrid lily developed in 1974 by California lily breeder Leslie Woodriff, who reportedly said of the upward-facing flowers 'it's looking at the stars,' giving the variety its name. Before Stargazer, Oriental lilies typically produced downward-facing blooms (like the tiger lily form), but Stargazer's upright, upward-facing flowers were a breakthrough — placing the spectacular blooms where they could be seen clearly and making them dramatically more impactful in the garden. The individual flowers are among the most beautiful in the plant kingdom: 6–8 inches across, with 6 recurved tepals in deep carmine-pink with white edges and margins, heavily spotted with dark maroon spots, and a deep red central stripe — the combination of colors, spots, and form creating an appearance of almost artificial perfection. The intense fragrance is as remarkable as the appearance: sweet, heavy, exotic, and pervasive — a single vase of Stargazer can fill an entire room with fragrance. Stargazer has remained the dominant Oriental lily after 50 years of continuous commercial production.
sources: American Lily Society — Stargazer lily development by Leslie Woodriff
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Oriental lily bulb variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bulb Lily Production in the Gulf South
